FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 21-site longitudinal study, ABCD Data Release 5.0; n=1,463 participants at Year 4 follow-up (mean age 14).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: describes and validates passively-sensed smartphone keyboard and app-use measures (reliability/ICC, convergent validity against self-report), and examines differences in smartphone use by sex and OS using t-tests and beta regressions.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Scientific Reports (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The paper reports quantitative estimates pairing smartphone variables (average daily keystrokes, average daily app use, self-reported smartphone use) with sex (t-tests: females > males, t_{1252.3}=6.9, p<0.001) and OS (Android vs iOS). All analyses examine associations between smartphone measures and demographics only (sex, OS, race/ethnicity, household income, parental education). No individual-trait-level variable (mental health, cognition, behavior, sleep, etc.) appears on the other side. Step 2 not reached. This is a pure psychometric/validation paper with demographic-only comparisons — other side is demographic-only throughout.

notes: Paper validates ABCD-EARS passive sensing smartphone measures and describes usage trends. All inferential analyses pair smartphone variables with demographics (sex, OS, race/ethnicity, income, parental education). No individual-trait outcome analyzed. Excluded as FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper describes NowIKnowMyABCD, a resource hub for researchers using U.S. ABCD Study data; explicitly describes U.S. ABCD Study as multisite longitudinal study of 11,876 children ages 9-10 across 21 data collection sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Paper is a resource/community-resource description paper presenting NowIKnowMyABCD (a GitHub discussion board and resource website for ABCD researchers). It reports no original empirical analysis of ABCD data. The paper describes the platform's features, categories, and a Twitter/X scraping pipeline but contains no inferential statistical analyses of ABCD participant data.

notes: Resource/community paper describing a centralized documentation and discussion platform for ABCD researchers. No empirical analyses of ABCD data. Excluded as FT-EC3.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,868, 21 U.S. sites, longitudinal follow-up ages 9/10 to 13/14 (waves 0-4).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using descriptive statistics, multilevel logistic regression (cross-sectional and longitudinal), and child-parent agreement analyses across five waves of ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The paper reports separate estimates for qualifying SM/phone variables (social media: OR=3.34, 95% CI: 3.14-3.55; video chat: OR=1.80; texting: OR=1.95). However, the other side of all analyses consists exclusively of demographic variables: biological sex, parent education, parent income, and parent marital status (Table 2). The paper explicitly states it examines associations between technology subtypes and sociodemographics. No individual-trait-level variable (mental health, cognition, behavior, sleep, well-being, etc.) appears on the other side of any analysis. Step 2 not applicable since Step 1 found qualifying SM variables but the other-side rule is not met.

notes: Paper examines longitudinal use patterns of technology subtypes (social media, texting, video chat, TV, video games, YouTube) and their associations with sociodemographics only (sex, parental education, income, marital status). No individual-trait outcome on the other side of any analysis. Excluded as FT-EC2.
